text: ‘Has either side the advantage?’; the Greek word here may mean ‘Do we excel them?’ or ‘Are we excelled by them?’ Nor are commentators agreed whether ‘we’ refers to the Jews or to the Gentiles. But the general sense remains clear.
‘The law’ seems to be used here, as often, for the Old Testament generally.
‘God’s praise’; some translate ‘the glory of God’, but it seems simplest to understand the words here as in Jn. 12.43, as referring to the praise which God bestows.

The Greek text here can be, and commonly is, translated thus: ‘he has also vindicated his (God’s) holiness, here and now, as one who is himself holy, and imparts holiness to those who take their stand upon faith in Jesus Christ’. The other interpretation is, apparently, that of the Latin translators.
As in verse 19 above, ‘the law’ seems to indicate the Old Testament generally, since the considerations here introduced relate to the story of Abraham.
